A History of Mind

Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Early Modern

John Locke

1632 – 1704

Ideas, consciousness, and the invention of personal identity as a forensic concept.

Locke does not write a single systematic “philosophy of mind” in the modern sense. What he gives instead is a dispersed but remarkably coherent account of ideas, consciousness, reflection, mental operations, agency, personal identity, spirit-substance, divine mind, and the possible minds of angels or other finite spirits. The central reconstruction I would propose is this:

Locke’s philosophy of mind is a theistic, empiricist, anti-Cartesian, substance-humble account of mindedness. It relocates philosophical certainty away from the hidden essence of the soul and toward observable or reflectable mental operations: perceiving, remembering, comparing, abstracting, willing, desiring, suspending desire, judging, and consciously appropriating actions as one’s own. Yet this is not a secular reduction of mind to psychology. God’s mind, divine omnipotence, resurrection, final judgment, and the possibility of angelic or otherwise non-human spirits are not marginal ornaments; they define the modal, moral, and metaphysical space within which Locke thinks finite minds must be understood.

In compressed form: Locke rejects innate contents; he rejects the Cartesian thesis that the soul’s essence is always-thinking; he denies that we have demonstrative knowledge of the soul’s immateriality; he allows that God could “superadd” thought to matter; he nevertheless argues that the first eternal source of thought must itself be a knowing, cogitative being; he treats human personhood as a forensic identity grounded in consciousness rather than in sameness of soul-substance; and he leaves room for a plurality of finite spirits above us, including angels, whose modes of thought and communication may surpass ours. These claims belong together. They form a philosophy of mind governed by empiricism, theological modesty, divine omnipotence, moral accountability, and a disciplined refusal to pretend to know the unknown substratum of spirit.

1. The textual field

The main sources are the Essay concerning Human Understanding, especially Book I on anti-innatism; Book II on ideas, reflection, mental operations, power, substance, and personal identity; Book IV on knowledge, divine existence, thinking matter, faith, reason, and the classification of the sciences; and Locke’s later exchange with Edward Stillingfleet, where the controversy over “thinking matter” becomes explicit. The personal identity chapter, II.xxvii, was added in the second edition of 1694 after William Molyneux urged Locke to treat the problem; it became one of the most heavily debated texts in early modern philosophy.

A reconstruction of Locke’s philosophy of mind must therefore avoid two distortions. The first is to make him a simple Cartesian dualist. He is not. He refuses to identify the essence of soul with thought, refuses to prove the immateriality of finite souls, and refuses to make sameness of soul the criterion of personal identity. The second distortion is to make him a straightforward materialist. He is not that either. He argues for an eternal knowing God, regards immaterial soul as at least highly probable, insists that matter cannot be the original source of thought by its own resources, and allows thinking matter only as something God could bestow by omnipotent power. Locke’s own view is more carefully described as agnostic about the substratum of finite mind, theistic about the ultimate source of mind, and operational or consciousness-based about what we can know of mindedness.

2. Locke’s basic vocabulary: idea, mind, soul, spirit, consciousness, person

Locke begins from “ideas,” by which he means whatever is the immediate object of understanding when a person thinks. His “idea” is deliberately broad: it covers sensory qualities, remembered images, abstract concepts, operations of the mind, relational notions, moral ideas, and ideas of substances. This broad use matters because Locke’s theory of mind begins not with a theory of soul-substance but with the inventory and origin of what appears before the understanding.

“Mind” and “understanding” name the subject or power that has, compares, compounds, abstracts, retains, and judges ideas. “Soul” and “spirit” name the thinking substance or supposed substratum underlying mental operations. Locke does not deny that there is such a substance. He denies that we have a clear idea of its inner essence. Our idea of spirit is built from reflection on operations such as thinking, willing, knowing, and initiating motion; but the substratum in which these powers inhere remains obscure. This is parallel to body: we know solidity, extension, motion, and cohesion, but not the ultimate substratum of corporeal substance.

“Consciousness” is more precise. For Locke, consciousness is inseparable from thinking: it is the mind’s awareness of its own perception or operation. The key point is not merely that I have mental states, but that I am aware of them as mine. Consciousness therefore becomes the ground of personal identity, moral appropriation, and responsibility. A “person” is not just a human organism, not simply a soul, and not merely a rational animal. It is a thinking intelligent being that can consider itself as itself, the same thinking thing, at different times and places.

This vocabulary already reveals Locke’s philosophical shift. Traditional metaphysics often asked, “What is the soul?” Locke asks first, “What do we experience, reflect upon, and have reason to affirm about thinking?” He does not abolish the soul; he brackets claims about its unknown essence and builds from ideas, operations, consciousness, and accountability.

3. Anti-innatism: the mind is not born furnished with principles

Locke’s anti-innatism is not a minor epistemological prologue. It is a founding move in his philosophy of mind. If there are no innate speculative or practical principles, then the mind’s furniture must be explained developmentally, through experience, attention, memory, abstraction, and language. The mind is not an already-inscribed repository of propositions. Locke repeatedly argues that for something to be “in the mind” in the relevant sense, it must be perceived, known, remembered, or at least be capable of being brought into consciousness as something previously perceived. The claim that a proposition is in the mind but never perceived is, for Locke, close to incoherent.

This has direct consequences for the soul. If the soul were born with innate ideas, then the soul would carry cognitive contents prior to experience. Locke denies this. The early human mind is not metaphysically empty in the sense of lacking all capacities, but it is epistemically unfurnished. His famous “white paper” image means that the materials of knowledge come from experience, not that the mind lacks all powers. Those powers become operative through sensation and reflection.

Locke’s anti-innatism also includes moral and religious implications. He denies innate practical principles, but not because he denies moral truth or God. Rather, he distinguishes innate truths from natural inclinations and from truths discoverable by reason. Desires for happiness and aversions to misery may be natural inclinations, but they are not innate propositions written into the understanding. This is crucial for his later account of agency: human beings are moved by uneasiness and desire, but rational responsibility depends on suspending desire, examining goods, and ordering conduct toward true happiness.

4. The two sources of mental content: sensation and reflection

Locke’s empiricism is dual-source empiricism. Ideas come from sensation, which supplies ideas of external sensible qualities, and reflection, which supplies ideas of the mind’s own operations. Reflection is sometimes misunderstood as introspection in a purely modern psychological sense. It is better understood as an internal sense: the mind notices what it does when it perceives, doubts, believes, reasons, knows, wills, desires, remembers, compares, and so on.

This is the basis of Locke’s philosophy of mind because the very idea of mind is not innate. We get our idea of mind by reflecting on mental operations. We know thinking not by peering into an immaterial essence, but by attending to acts of perception, volition, reasoning, and consciousness. Reflection therefore plays two roles. It supplies ideas of mental operations, and it also makes possible the idea of spirit as a substance that performs such operations.

Locke’s account is also developmental. Reflection does not necessarily appear in full force at the beginning of life. Children first receive sensory ideas, and only later, by attention, become aware of the mind’s operations on those ideas. Reflection requires a turn of attention toward mental activity itself. This is one reason Locke rejects the idea that the soul must always have been thinking in a way accessible to itself.

Once simple ideas are received, the mind is not merely passive. It can repeat, compare, unite, separate, compound, abstract, and name them. Locke’s empiricism is therefore not a crude copy theory in which the mind only receives impressions. The simple materials are given by experience; complex ideas, classifications, moral concepts, substances, modes, and abstract ideas are actively constructed by the understanding.

5. Locke against the always-thinking soul

One of Locke’s most important anti-Cartesian claims is that the soul does not always think, or at least that we have no reason to affirm that it does. He does not deny the soul’s existence during dreamless sleep. He denies that thought can occur in the soul while entirely outside consciousness and still count, for us, as the soul’s thinking. Locke’s challenge is pointed: if a sleeping person has thoughts of which waking consciousness has no awareness, what makes those thoughts belong to that person?

The contrast with Descartes is fundamental. For Descartes, thought is the essence of mind. For Locke, thinking is an operation of the mind, not its essence. The soul may be capable of thought without always thinking, just as body may be capable of motion without always moving. Locke even suggests that perpetual thinking may belong properly to God, not to finite souls. This point both lowers metaphysical confidence about human soul-substance and elevates God as the possible paradigm of unceasing intellectual actuality.

Locke’s dreamless-sleep argument also connects to duration and personal identity. Our idea of duration arises from the train or succession of ideas. Where there is no conscious succession, as in dreamless sleep, there is no perceived duration for the person. This does not mean time stops or the organism ceases; it means that personal consciousness is intermittent in a way that a theory of mind must acknowledge.

Spanish-language pedagogical and interpretive materials often highlight this anti-Cartesian point: Locke attacks the claim that thinking is the essence of the soul and denies that the soul always thinks. That reception is useful because it keeps the mind/soul distinction visible: Locke is not denying thinking, but detaching it from an alleged essence known a priori.

6. Mental operations: perception, memory, discernment, comparison, abstraction

Locke’s positive psychology begins with perception. Perception is the first simple idea of reflection and the first operation of the understanding. It is not reducible to bodily impulse. Organic stimulation alone does not make perception unless the mind notices or receives the impression. At the same time, Locke does not make perception a purely active construction. Bare perception is mostly passive: the mind receives ideas.

Perception also marks the boundary between animals and merely vegetative nature. Locke attributes perception to animals in degrees. He does not reserve all mentality for human beings. Animals perceive, remember, and in some cases compare and compound ideas, though they do not reach the human capacity for abstraction and general reasoning. The philosophy of mind implied here is hierarchical rather than binary: there are degrees and kinds of mental operation across living beings.

Memory or retention is the mind’s power to revive past perceptions with an awareness that they have been had before. This “as past” structure matters deeply. Memory is not merely the recurrence of an image; it is the revival of an idea accompanied by recognition. The strength of memory depends on attention, repetition, pleasure, pain, and the original vividness of the impression. Ideas fade, and the mind’s continuity is therefore psychologically fragile.

Comparison, compounding, and abstraction form the constructive side of Locke’s account. The mind compares ideas to perceive relations; compounds simple ideas into complex ones; and abstracts from particulars to form general ideas. Locke’s empiricism therefore gives us a theory of concept formation. Even highly abstruse ideas are made by repeating and joining simple ideas originally obtained from sensation and reflection.

Attention plays a special role. Locke recognizes that the mind can be so absorbed in some ideas that ordinary sensory impressions go unnoticed, while at other times ideas pass faintly and without firm retention. This gives Locke a proto-psychology of attention, distraction, intensity, and cognitive salience.

7. Faculties are powers, not little agents inside the mind

Locke is careful about faculty language. He speaks of understanding, will, memory, and abstraction, but he resists treating faculties as independent agents. It is not “the will” that chooses as though the will were a person inside the person. The person, mind, or agent acts by exercising powers. Faculties are relations or capacities, not homunculi.

This anti-reification of faculties is central to Locke’s philosophy of mind. It avoids a common scholastic or pseudo-explanatory move: explaining action by naming a power, then treating the named power as if it did the action. For Locke, “will,” “understanding,” and “memory” are useful abstractions, but the real explanatory target is the operation of the agent. The mind perceives; the person wills; the agent suspends desire; the understanding compares ideas.

The result is a disciplined faculty psychology. Locke neither eliminates faculties nor personifies them. He treats them as powers grounded in substances whose substrata we do not fully know. This is consistent with his wider metaphysical humility: we know powers and operations better than essences.

8. Pleasure, pain, uneasiness, desire, and the practical mind

Locke’s mind is not merely cognitive. It is affective and practical. Pleasure and pain enter both through sensation and reflection; they are tied to bodily experience and to mental delight or uneasiness. Human motivation is structured by desire, and desire is closely linked to uneasiness. Locke famously argues that the will is ordinarily determined not by the greatest abstract good but by the most pressing present uneasiness.

This is a psychologically realistic doctrine. We may judge a future good to be greater and still act on a present uneasiness. Addiction, fear, anger, lust, ambition, and anxiety can dominate practical cognition. Locke’s theory of mind therefore includes a theory of akrasia or weakness of will, but without treating the will as a separate faculty that mysteriously breaks loose from reason. The agent acts under the pressure of present desire unless he can suspend action and deliberate.

The power of suspension is the pivot of Locke’s account of liberty. A finite rational being is free not because the will is undetermined in some abstract metaphysical sense, but because the person can pause before satisfying desire, examine the proposed good, compare it with long-term happiness, and redirect conduct. In this suspension Locke locates human liberty and moral responsibility.

God enters this account not as a decorative theological addition but as a contrast case. Locke says God is under a necessity of being happy; finite intellectual beings, by contrast, must deliberate under incomplete knowledge, competing desires, and the possibility of error. The future state, divine judgment, and the immortality or restoration of the person affect practical reasoning because eternal happiness and misery radically alter the rational weight of choices.

9. Liberty: not free will, but free agents

Locke’s treatment of freedom is deliberately deflationary about “free will.” The right question is not whether the will is free, but whether the person is free to act or forbear according to the mind’s preference. Liberty belongs to agents in relation to actions, not to the will considered as an isolated faculty.

His locked-room example is designed to show that voluntariness and liberty are not identical. A person may choose to remain in a room without knowing the door is locked; the stay is voluntary, but not free in the relevant respect, because the person lacks the power to leave. Liberty requires the power to do or not do according to preference. In the mental case, liberty includes the power to direct attention, take up or lay aside thoughts, and suspend the satisfaction of desire—though pain, passion, and obsession can constrain even this internal freedom.

The philosophy of mind extracted from this is action-oriented. Mind is not just a theater of ideas; it is a power-bearing center of attention, preference, suspension, and practical self-government. But Locke refuses to exaggerate our autonomy. Human agency is finite, pressured, embodied, habituated, and often self-deceived.

10. Association of ideas: Locke’s cognitive pathology

Locke’s chapter on the association of ideas is one of his most important contributions to philosophical psychology. Ideas that have no natural connection can become linked by chance, custom, education, trauma, passion, or repetition. Once linked, they guide inference, feeling, action, prejudice, and error.

This doctrine anticipates later associationist psychology, but in Locke it is also moral and epistemic. Bad associations produce irrational aversions, sectarian conviction, superstitions, personal antipathies, and false systems. Education can settle these connections early, making them feel obvious or necessary. Locke thereby explains how sincere people may reason badly while experiencing their errors as self-evident.

His discussion of faith, reason, and enthusiasm belongs here. Locke accepts divine revelation, but he insists that reason must judge whether a purported revelation really is revelation. Without this check, enthusiasm becomes indistinguishable from inspiration, and private conviction can override all evidence. This is not anti-religious; it is a theory of responsible religious cognition.

11. Substance: body and spirit are equally obscure at the substratum level

Locke’s account of substance is essential for understanding his view of soul. We have ideas of qualities and powers, then suppose a “something” in which they inhere. This supposition gives us the idea of substance in general, but it does not give us clear knowledge of the substratum. This applies equally to material and spiritual substance.

Our idea of body is built from solidity, extension, figure, motion, rest, cohesion, and impulse. Our idea of spirit is built from thinking, understanding, willing, knowing, liberty, and the power to begin motion. We are not entitled to say that body is perfectly intelligible while spirit is mysterious; nor are we entitled to say that spirit is perfectly intelligible while body is obvious. Both are known through collections of ideas and powers, while their inner substrata remain hidden.

This symmetry is one of Locke’s deepest anti-dogmatic moves. It blocks materialist triumphalism because body is not transparently known. It also blocks dogmatic dualism because spirit is not transparently known. Locke’s position is not “we know matter, but not mind”; nor “we know mind, but not matter.” It is: we know ideas, operations, powers, and relations; we do not know the real essences of either body or spirit.

12. Thinking matter: Locke’s controlled but radical possibility

The famous “thinking matter” thesis must be stated with care. Locke does not claim that matter in fact thinks by its own nature. He claims that we cannot demonstrate that God could not give suitably disposed matter the power to perceive and think. God might either join an immaterial thinking substance to body or superadd the power of thought to matter. Human beings do not have demonstrative knowledge sufficient to exclude either possibility.

This claim shocked many contemporaries because it seemed to endanger the immaterial soul. Locke’s reply is subtle. Morality and religion do not require philosophers to prove the soul’s immateriality. God can preserve, restore, or resurrect sensible intelligent beings for reward and punishment whether the finite thinking subject is immaterial or whether God has bestowed thought on matter. The practical and religious issue is not the metaphysical stuff of the soul but God’s power to continue or restore the conscious person.

In the Stillingfleet correspondence Locke clarifies that he is defending divine omnipotence and epistemic modesty, not crude materialism. He insists that matter cannot originally have perception and knowledge from itself; his point is that God may superadd thinking to matter. He repeatedly argues that inability to conceive how matter could think is a limitation of our understanding, not a limitation on God’s power.

This is a key place where theology is doing philosophical work. Locke’s thinking-matter possibility depends on divine omnipotence. The modal claim is not “matter can think naturally”; it is “we cannot know that God cannot make matter think.” This distinction allows Locke to avoid both Cartesian dogmatism and atheistic materialism. It also places the philosophy of mind inside a doctrine of creation: mental powers may be bestowed powers, not transparent consequences of a substance’s known essence.

Recent English-language scholarship continues to debate how far this goes. Some interpreters treat Locke as a cautious agnostic who puts dualism and materialism on an epistemic par, while others argue that he was more sympathetic to material minds than his surface formulations admit. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s discussion emphasizes Locke’s refusal to decide demonstratively between dualist and materialist accounts of finite minds, while noting that he regarded moral and religious doctrine as secure either way.

13. The soul’s immateriality: probable, not demonstrable

Locke’s position on the immaterial soul is therefore not denial but demotion from certainty to probability. He can regard immateriality as highly probable while refusing to claim demonstration. In the correspondence with Stillingfleet, he explicitly resists the accusation that allowing divine superaddition makes him a materialist or atheist. His view is that no philosophical proof has shown contradiction in God’s giving thought to matter, and therefore no demonstration of finite soul-immateriality is available.

This matters for personal identity. If personal identity depended on sameness of immaterial soul, then we would need a way to identify the same soul across time, sleep, memory loss, resurrection, and possible transfer. Locke thinks we lack such knowledge. The soul may be the same, but sameness of soul is not what makes the person the same for consciousness, responsibility, or self-concern.

Here Locke’s philosophy is neither anti-soul nor soul-centered. The soul remains possible, probable, and theologically significant. But it is not the epistemic foundation of mind, nor the criterion of personhood. The center of gravity shifts from substance to consciousness.

14. The mind of God: the necessary eternal cogitative being

Locke’s treatment of God is indispensable to his philosophy of mind. In Book IV, he argues that we have intuitive knowledge of our own existence, demonstrative knowledge of God, and sensitive knowledge of external things. The proof of God begins from the certainty that I exist, the principle that nothing cannot produce something, and the fact that knowledge or perception exists in us. From this he argues that there must be an eternal, most powerful, most knowing being.

The decisive anti-materialist premise is not that matter cannot be made to think by God. Locke allows that possibility. The premise is that incogitative matter, left to itself as the first eternal thing, cannot produce cogitative being. Thought cannot originate from what is wholly without thought, power, and direction. Matter and motion, without a prior knowing cause, cannot generate knowledge. Thus Locke distinguishes two questions: whether matter can receive thought from God, and whether matter can be the original self-sufficient source of thought. He denies the second while leaving the first open.

This distinction is the backbone of Locke’s theistic philosophy of mind. Finite thought may be metaphysically realized in ways we cannot determine. But the ultimate source of thought must be an eternal mind. God is not merely another item in Locke’s ontology; God is the condition under which mental powers, whether in immaterial souls or thinking matter, are intelligible as created powers.

Locke’s idea of God is formed by enlarging ideas gained from reflection and sensation: existence, duration, knowledge, power, pleasure or happiness, and so forth are expanded toward infinity. This idea is inadequate to God’s real essence, but it is not meaningless. It is an analogical and augmentative idea: we begin from finite mental operations and remove limits as far as we can.

God also functions as the supreme case of intellectual perfection. Locke’s remark that God is under a necessity of being happy is not a casual devotional line. It marks the difference between divine and finite mindedness. God’s mind is not beset by ignorance, passion, uneasiness, or misjudgment. Finite minds must deliberate, suspend desire, and seek happiness under conditions of limitation.

Finally, God is crucial for personal identity and justice. Locke’s forensic theory of personhood depends on the thought that divine judgment can assign responsibility according to true consciousness, not merely according to bodily evidence. Human law must often punish drunken or forgotten actions because it cannot reliably inspect consciousness; divine judgment can reveal the heart and will not punish a person for what the person is wholly unconscious of having done.

15. Angels and finite spirits: not distractions, but boundary cases

Locke’s remarks on angels and finite spirits are sparse but philosophically important. They show that his account of mind is not anthropocentric in a narrow sense. Human minds are one kind of finite mind, not the measure of all mindedness.

Our ideas of spirits, including angels, are built from reflection on our own mental operations. We have ideas of thinking, willing, knowing, and active power; we can combine and enlarge these to form ideas of spirits superior to us. But our natural faculties do not give us clear knowledge of the existence or inner constitution of particular angels. Locke allows that we may believe in finite spirits on revelation or probable grounds, but we do not know their existence by sensation in the way we know external bodies, nor by intuition in the way we know ourselves.

Locke also speculates that separate spirits may communicate thoughts more perfectly than we do. Human beings rely on bodily signs, especially words, because our thoughts are not immediately open to one another. Angels or separate spirits may have modes of communication beyond our imagination. This is not a throwaway theological fantasy. It reveals that Locke’s philosophy of mind treats linguistic communication as contingent on human embodiment, not as essential to all intelligence.

In Book IV’s division of the sciences, Locke includes God, angels, spirits, and bodies within natural philosophy, broadly understood as the knowledge of things. He then distinguishes this from ethics or practical philosophy and from semiotics, the doctrine of signs, because things besides the mind itself are not immediately present to the understanding. This classification confirms that angels and spirits are part of Locke’s philosophical ontology, even if our knowledge of them is limited.

The philosophical lesson is that Locke’s empiricism is not a denial of suprasensible minds. It is a rule about the origin and limits of our ideas. We may have meaningful but inadequate ideas of God and spirits by reflecting on our own mental operations and enlarging them; we may believe more by revelation; but we should not pretend to possess clear natural knowledge of angelic psychology.

16. Human minds, animal minds, and the parrot problem

Locke’s treatment of animals complicates any simple equation of mind with human rationality. Animals have perception and memory. Some compare and compound ideas. They act intelligently in limited ways. What they lack, in Locke’s standard account, is the full power of abstraction that enables general ideas and language-governed reasoning.

The famous rational parrot example presses on the boundary between animal, human, and person. Locke imagines a parrot capable of rational conversation. Such a being would still be called a parrot in one classificatory sense, while a dull irrational human would still be called a man in another. This shows Locke’s method: identity depends on the sortal term under which we classify. “Animal,” “man,” “person,” and “spirit” are not interchangeable.

This matters for contemporary extraction. Locke does not define personhood by biological humanity alone. A non-human rational conscious being could potentially count as a person; a human organism is not automatically the same philosophical category as a person considered for moral responsibility. This is why angels matter too. They are possible non-human rational minds and therefore help reveal the generality of Locke’s concept of person.

Recent Portuguese-language work has returned to Locke’s human/animal boundary, including the parrot case, as part of the question of animality and humanity in seventeenth-century debates. That line of reception is valuable because it keeps Locke’s personal-identity theory from being read solely as a human memory puzzle; it is also a theory of classificatory kinds and non-human mindedness.

17. Personal identity: same man, same substance, same person

Locke’s personal identity theory begins with a distinction among kinds. The identity conditions of a mass of matter, a living plant, an animal, a human organism, a soul, and a person are different. A living organism remains the same through organized continuity of life, despite material replacement. A “man” may be defined by living human organization. But a “person” is a thinking intelligent being that can consider itself as itself through time.

Personal identity consists in the extension of consciousness backward to past actions and thoughts. I am the same person as the one who performed a past action insofar as present consciousness can appropriate that action as mine. This does not require sameness of bodily matter. It also does not require demonstrable sameness of soul-substance. The same consciousness could, in principle, be transferred from one thinking substance to another; conversely, the same soul without continuity of consciousness would not preserve the same person in the relevant forensic sense.

Locke’s theory is often summarized as a memory theory, but “memory theory” can be misleading if it suggests a narrow archive of episodic recollections. Consciousness is broader than memory alone. Memory is the chief way consciousness extends backward, but the real criterion is conscious appropriation: the capacity to own past actions and thoughts as belonging to oneself. That is why Locke describes “person” as a forensic term. It concerns responsibility, concernment, merit, guilt, reward, and punishment.

The theological dimension is not incidental. Divine judgment supplies the ideal forensic context. Human courts rely on evidence and may punish actions performed while drunk because they cannot know whether consciousness extended to them. God, by contrast, can judge according to the true reach of consciousness. Locke’s theory is therefore not merely psychological; it is juridical and eschatological.

The Stanford Encyclopedia emphasizes that Locke’s same-soul criterion is neither necessary nor sufficient for personal identity: the same soul without consciousness of past actions would not be the same person, while the same consciousness transferred to another substance would preserve personal identity. It also highlights the later objections of Butler and Reid, including worries about circularity and transitivity, and the role of early defender Catharine Cockburn.

18. Resurrection and the forensic self

Locke’s personal identity theory should be read together with his remarks on resurrection and divine justice. If God can restore the same consciousness, then God can restore the same person for judgment even if the material or substantial basis is not what philosophers expected. This is why Locke’s refusal to prove soul-immateriality does not, in his eyes, undermine Christianity or morality. The person who is accountable is the conscious self, not an unknown substratum considered apart from consciousness.

This does not mean the soul is irrelevant. It means that the soul is not the criterion by which responsibility is assigned. The soul may be the vehicle of consciousness; it may be immaterial; it may be preserved by God; but unless consciousness extends to the action, forensic personhood does not attach. Locke’s deepest move is to separate metaphysical survival of substance from moral survival of the person.

This is why “person” for Locke is practical before it is metaphysical. The self is the being that is concerned for its own happiness and misery. The reach of selfhood follows the reach of consciousness because concernment follows consciousness. To punish a being for an action it cannot in any way consciously appropriate would, for Locke, be equivalent to creating a miserable being rather than justly punishing the guilty person.

19. Knowledge of self, God, other minds, and finite spirits

Locke’s epistemology of minds has a graded structure. I know my own existence intuitively. I know God demonstratively. I know external things by sensation. I know other human minds indirectly, by words, actions, and signs. I do not have natural sensitive knowledge of angels or particular finite spirits unless they somehow reveal themselves; I may believe in them through revelation or probability.

This is a powerful architecture. Self-knowledge is not knowledge of the soul’s essence; it is consciousness of my own existence in thinking, sensing, reasoning, and perceiving. God is known by demonstration from the existence of finite knowing beings and the impossibility that knowledge arise from nothing or from purely incogitative matter as first cause. Other finite minds are known by signs. Angelic minds are conceptually possible and theologically credible but not naturally inspectable.

Semiotics is therefore inseparable from Locke’s philosophy of mind. Since another person’s thoughts are not immediately present to my understanding, signs are required. Words are public instruments for communicating private ideas. This dependence on signs also explains how misunderstanding, verbal confusion, sectarianism, and empty scholastic terms can corrupt thought.

20. Language, signs, and the social mind

Locke’s doctrine of signs is not merely a philosophy of language appended to a theory of ideas. It is part of the theory of mind’s social condition. Human minds are private in the sense that ideas are immediately present only to the thinker. Communication therefore depends on signs, especially words, whose meanings are conventional and often unstable. This makes knowledge socially fragile.

For embodied human minds, language allows abstraction, classification, moral reasoning, law, theology, and science. But it also enables empty disputes. People may use words without clear ideas, inherit party slogans, or profess opinions they have not truly examined. Locke’s analysis of wrong assent and partisan belief shows how social identity can colonize cognition.

The possible minds of angels again matter by contrast. If separate spirits communicate without bodily signs, then human language is not the essence of thought as such. It is the embodied human solution to the privacy of ideas. Locke therefore has room for minded beings whose communication is more immediate or more perfect than ours.

21. Non-English reception and interpretive angles

Several non-English discussions bring out features of Locke that are sometimes flattened in Anglophone summaries.

In French reception, Pierre Coste’s translation and presentation were philosophically consequential. Delphine Soulard argues that Coste foregrounded the issue of “matière pensante,” even though it was only one element of Stillingfleet’s objections, and that his later presentation shifted the problem away from the immediate English religious context toward a broader French and European debate in which materialist or irreligious implications became more visible. Soulard also emphasizes Coste’s close collaboration with Locke and the dual character of the translation: clarifying and “Frenchifying” the text while remaining unusually faithful to Locke.

French scholarship on “conscience” is also important. Work discussed by Delphine Kolesnik-Antoine and others, drawing on Étienne Balibar, stresses that Coste’s use of conscience helped introduce into French philosophy a non-moral sense of consciousness as self-awareness of one’s actions and thoughts through memory. That point is highly relevant because Locke’s “consciousness” is not simply moral conscience; it is reflexive awareness and appropriation.

Philippe Hamou’s work, discussed in Laurent Jaffro’s review of Dans la chambre obscure de l’esprit. John Locke et l’invention du mind, frames Locke as central to the “invention” of the modern mind and treats thinking matter as a major interpretive problem. Hamou is associated with the stronger thesis that Locke probably judged the materiality of the human soul to be likely, not merely possible. That is a contested reading, but it is valuable because it presses the radical edge of Locke’s epistemic humility.

Voltaire’s Spanish-accessible presentation of Locke is revealing for eighteenth-century continental reception. Voltaire portrays Locke as modestly asking whether we can know that a material being cannot think, and he insists that the Creator’s ability to give thought to matter is a philosophical question that should not be settled by dogmatic outrage. He also distinguishes the religious question of immortality, settled by revelation, from the philosophical question of the soul’s substance.

Spanish-language discussions of personal identity often emphasize that Locke replaces the Cartesian res cogitans or substance-based foundation with consciousness, while raising problems about circularity and the unknown substance that might guarantee identity. These interpretations rightly connect the personal identity theory with Locke’s broader critique of substance.

German-language discussions frequently stress Locke’s anti-Cartesian move: he opposes the thesis that body’s essence is extension and spirit’s essence is thinking; he treats thinking as a detachable operation or function rather than an essence, and he uses dreamless sleep against the doctrine that the soul always thinks. Another German discussion emphasizes that Locke’s consciousness is an empirical-reflexive fact rather than the Cartesian thinking substance itself.

Italian and Portuguese reception adds two useful emphases. Italian materials often present Locke as detaching personal identity from the metaphysics of soul-substance—hence themes such as “after the soul”—and making consciousness central. Portuguese work has explored the relation between personal identity and justice, arguing that Locke’s dependence of justice on consciousness generates hard cases, and has also returned to the human/animal boundary through the parrot and animality problem.

The cumulative lesson from these receptions is that Locke’s philosophy of mind has at least four live interpretive centers: consciousness rather than substance; thinking matter and divine omnipotence; the anti-Cartesian denial that the soul always thinks; and the forensic-moral structure of personal identity. Different linguistic traditions emphasize different nodes in that structure.

22. Extracted Lockean philosophy of mind: systematic theses

The following theses give the most comprehensive reconstruction.

First, all human mental content derives from experience. Sensation gives ideas of external things; reflection gives ideas of mental operations. There are no innate propositions or innate ideas in the strict epistemic sense. The mind has native capacities, but not innate cognitive furniture.

Second, the mind is known operationally, not essentially. We know perceiving, remembering, comparing, willing, desiring, and reasoning; we do not know the real essence of the substance in which these occur. This applies to spirit as much as to body.

Third, consciousness is actual and reflexive. Thinking is not hidden occurrence wholly outside awareness. Consciousness accompanies thought and enables the thinker to appropriate thoughts and actions as its own. This is why dreamless sleep is philosophically significant.

Fourth, thinking is an operation of the soul, not demonstrably its essence. Locke rejects the Cartesian always-thinking soul. The soul may exist when not thinking, just as a body may exist when not moving.

Fifth, simple ideas are passively received, but the mind actively forms complex ideas. The understanding repeats, compares, compounds, abstracts, and names. Locke’s empiricism is therefore compatible with a robust theory of mental construction.

Sixth, faculties are powers, not agents. Locke rejects explanations in which “the will” or “the understanding” acts like a miniature person. The person or mind acts by exercising powers.

Seventh, finite minds are embodied, affective, and motivationally pressured. Desire, uneasiness, pleasure, pain, habit, and passion structure practical cognition. Rational freedom depends especially on the power to suspend desire and deliberate about happiness.

Eighth, mind-body metaphysics is underdetermined for finite creatures. We cannot demonstrate whether finite thought requires immaterial substance or whether God can bestow thought on matter. Both material and immaterial accounts of finite mind exceed our clear knowledge at the substratum level.

Ninth, matter cannot be the original source of thought by itself. Locke’s openness to thinking matter is not atheistic materialism, because he argues that the first eternal being must be cogitative. Incogitative matter cannot generate knowledge as the ultimate source.

Tenth, God’s mind is metaphysically foundational. God is the eternal knowing being from whom finite mental powers derive. God’s omnipotence grounds the possibility of superadded powers; God’s justice grounds the final forensic application of personal identity.

Eleventh, angelic and other finite spirits are philosophically meaningful boundary cases. They show that mindedness is not exhausted by human embodiment, human language, or human cognitive limits. We have only inadequate ideas of them, but those ideas are built legitimately from reflection and enlargement.

Twelfth, personal identity is forensic consciousness, not sameness of soul or body. A person is the same over time insofar as consciousness extends to past actions and thoughts. “Person” is a term of responsibility, concernment, merit, guilt, reward, and punishment.

Thirteenth, human cognition is socially and associatively vulnerable. Custom, education, language, party, passion, and chance associations shape belief. Locke’s theory of mind includes not only perception and reason but also error, prejudice, enthusiasm, and cognitive deformation.

23. Major tensions and interpretive problems

The first major tension is between consciousness as the criterion of personal identity and the apparent transitivity problem. If an old general remembers being a young officer, and the young officer remembered being a child, but the old general no longer remembers being the child, is the old general the same person as the child? Reid’s famous objection presses this issue. Locke’s defenders often answer by distinguishing direct memory from overlapping continuity of consciousness, but Locke’s own text leaves room for debate.

The second problem is circularity. Does memory constitute personal identity, or must memory already presuppose personal identity because genuine memory is memory of what I did? Butler pressed this objection. A Lockean answer is that consciousness does not infer identity from memory as an external proof; rather, conscious appropriation itself constitutes the forensic unity. But this answer is interpretive and remains contested.

The third problem concerns the scope of divine judgment. Locke solves evidential gaps by appealing to God’s perfect knowledge, but philosophically this means the final application of the theory depends on a theological premise. That is not a defect if one is reconstructing Locke faithfully; it is a feature. A secularized Lockean theory must replace the divine judge with some other account of truth about consciousness, responsibility, and forgotten action.

The fourth problem is thinking matter. Locke’s distinction between “matter cannot originate thought by itself” and “God could give thought to matter” is coherent, but unstable under pressure. More cautious interpreters read him as agnostic; more radical interpreters, especially in some French and materialist-oriented receptions, suspect that he leaned toward the probability of material human minds. The textual evidence supports at least agnosticism and modal openness; whether it supports probable materialism is a further interpretive question.

The fifth problem concerns angels and spirits. Locke gives us a principled way to form ideas of spirits by reflection and enlargement, but he also insists that our natural knowledge of particular finite spirits is limited. This means angelic minds are philosophically significant but epistemically underdescribed. They expand the modal map of mindedness without yielding a detailed angelic psychology.

The sixth problem is whether Locke’s actualist consciousness leaves room for unconscious mental states. He is hostile to the idea of thoughts wholly unknown to the thinker, especially when used to defend innate ideas or the always-thinking soul. Yet he recognizes faint trains of ideas, unnoticed sensory impressions, habitual judgments in perception, and associations operating beneath explicit rational control. Locke is not a Freudian theorist of the unconscious, but his psychology contains phenomena that later thinkers could develop in that direction.

24. Relation to later philosophy of mind

Locke’s position anticipates several later debates, but it should not be collapsed into any one of them.

Against Cartesian substance dualism, Locke denies that we know mind as an immaterial substance whose essence is thought. Against reductive materialism, he denies that matter by itself can be the ultimate source of thought and argues for an eternal knowing God. Against behaviorism, he treats consciousness, reflection, and private ideas as fundamental. Against simple introspectionism, he emphasizes error, association, language, habit, and the limits of self-knowledge. Against purely biological accounts of personhood, he defines personhood by consciousness and forensic responsibility. Against purely secular memory theories, he embeds personal identity in divine justice and resurrection.

A contemporary label such as “functionalist” is tempting but only partly accurate. Locke does individuate mind by operations and powers more than by hidden substrate, and he allows that different substrates might realize thought if God so orders them. That resembles functionalist flexibility. But Locke’s theory is not mechanistic functionalism: it is constrained by divine creation, moral accountability, consciousness, and the demonstrative priority of an eternal mind.

Likewise, “dualist” is partly accurate but misleading. Locke accepts spiritual substance and divine mind, and he thinks immaterial soul is probable. But he refuses to demonstrate finite soul-immateriality or to ground personhood in sameness of soul. “Substance-agnostic theistic mental realism” is clunky, but closer to the truth.

25. Final synthesis

Locke’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as a disciplined inquiry into finite thinking under divine conditions.

The finite human mind receives all its ideas from sensation and reflection. It knows its own operations better than its own essence. It is conscious when it thinks, but it does not always think. It constructs complex ideas, abstracts, compares, names, remembers, desires, wills, and judges. It is vulnerable to custom, association, enthusiasm, and verbal confusion. It is free chiefly in its power to suspend desire and deliberate about happiness. It is accountable as a person insofar as consciousness extends to actions and thoughts.

The soul is not discarded. It is treated with metaphysical humility. The soul may be immaterial; Locke thinks that probable. But we cannot demonstrate it. Nor can we use sameness of soul as the criterion of personal identity, because we lack access to the soul’s identity apart from consciousness. What matters for personhood is not an unknowable substratum but conscious self-appropriation under the horizon of responsibility.

God is indispensable. God is the eternal knowing being without whom finite thought cannot ultimately be explained. God’s omnipotence makes it possible that thought be joined to immaterial substance or superadded to matter. God’s justice secures the forensic truth of personal identity beyond the limits of human evidence. God’s infinite mind is formed in our thought by enlarging ideas of knowledge, power, duration, and happiness beyond finite limits, though the real divine essence remains beyond our adequate comprehension.

Angels and finite spirits are likewise important. They prevent us from mistaking human embodied cognition for the whole of mind. Locke’s philosophy allows orders of finite intelligence above us, different modes of communication, and forms of spiritual life known only inadequately by natural reason and more fully, if at all, by revelation. These are not distractions from philosophy of mind; they are part of Locke’s map of possible mindedness.

The resulting system is not Cartesian, not materialist, not purely theological, and not purely psychological. It is an empiricist and theistic philosophy of minded powers, conscious personhood, moral agency, and metaphysical humility. Its most radical move is to make consciousness, not soul-substance, the philosophical center of finite personhood—while still placing all finite mind within a world created, governed, and judged by the infinite mind of God.

PreviousBaruch Spinoza NextGottfried Wilhelm Leibniz

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
